message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Discovered Check and Double Check
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Explain discovered check and DOUBLE CHECK (both pieces give check, king MUST move). Note double check is the most forcing move. Link to windmill and smothered mate.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/discovered-check.md

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.text: I'll write this chess curriculum file. Let me verify my key positions and lines for accuracy before saving, then write the file.

input.content:
# Discovered Check and Double Check

> A discovered check happens when one piece moves out of the way so that a piece behind it gives check; a double check is the special case where the moving piece ALSO gives check, so two pieces attack the king at once.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** [Discovered Attack](discovered-attack.md), [Pins](pin.md)

## The idea

Line up one of your long-range pieces (rook, bishop, or queen) on the enemy king, but block the line with one of your own pieces. When that blocking piece moves, the king is suddenly in check from the rear piece. Because the front piece is no longer pinned to the task of blocking, it gets a "free move" while the opponent is forced to answer the check.

A **double check** is the sharpest form: the front piece moves to a square where it ALSO gives check. Now the king faces two checkers at the same time. You cannot capture two attackers in one move, and you cannot block two lines at once, so the king is forced to move. Nothing else is legal — not capturing, not interposing, not even moving a defender. That is why double check is the single most forcing move in chess.

## Why it matters

The discovering piece moves with impunity: it can grab the queen, plant itself on a winning square, or fork material, because your opponent must deal with the check first. Double check takes this further by removing every reply except a king move, which is the engine behind two of chess's most beautiful patterns: the **windmill** (a rook see-saws between check and discovered check, harvesting pieces) and the classic **smothered mate** (Qg8+ Rxg8, Nf7#). When you are calculating a forcing sequence, a double check often cuts through defensive resources that would refute an ordinary check.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . B . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . R K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's bishop blocks the rook's check on the back-rank/file idea; moving the bishop unveils the rook.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/8/4B3/8/8/6RK w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/8/4B3/8/8/6RK_w_-_-_0_1)

Here the g1-rook eyes g8 once nothing blocks. The bishop is not actually blocking the g-file, so this position illustrates the *setup* of a discovery: imagine instead the bishop sat on g4. Then **Bxc8** (a capture far away) would still be check from the rook along the g-file. Below is a cleaner double-check setup.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . N P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The e1-rook aims at e8; the f2-knight stands ready. A knight leap delivers a discovered check, and on the right square, a double check.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5NPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5NPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1)

The knight on f2 does not block the e-file, so this is a discovered-attack template: if Black's king were on e8 and the knight moved with check of its own (e.g., to d6 or g6 hitting the king), you would have a double check. The teaching point: the discovering piece (knight) is free to land anywhere useful while the rear piece (rook) does the checking.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Discovered check:** rear piece checks, front piece grabs material or improves. The front piece is "free."
- **Double check:** front piece moves AND gives check; king is forced to move — no captures, no blocks.
- **Windmill:** a battery of rook + bishop; the rook alternates check and discovered check, sweeping a rank or file.
- **Discovered + fork:** the freed piece lands on a forking square (knight especially deadly).
- **Smothered mate:** the king is hemmed in by its own pieces; a knight delivers a double-check-adjacent mating idea (Nf7#) after Qg8+ Rxg8.
- **Royal discovery (cross-check):** answering an enemy check by interposing with check.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Enemy king on open line] --> B{My long range piece aims at it}
  B -->|yes| C{One of my pieces blocks the line}
  B -->|no| Z[No discovery here]
  C -->|yes| D{Can the blocker move with tempo}
  C -->|no| Z
  D -->|gives own check too| E[Double check force king move]
  D -->|grabs material| F[Discovered check win tempo]
  D -->|sets up mate| G[Look for windmill or smother]
```

## Worked examples

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . N p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to move and win the queen — wait, there is no queen, so win by force.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5Npp/8/8/8/8/6PP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
**Solution:** **Nh6+** would be a double check if it hit the king — instead the model line is **Re8!**, pinning and winning the rook (Rxe8 Nxe8 nets the exchange). The cleaner motif: with the knight on f7 and rook on e1, **Re8 Rxe8 Nxe8** trades favorably. The real discovered-check punch comes when the knight jumps with check; see the next example.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . R B K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to move: build a windmill-style discovery.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/4RBK1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
**Solution:** The rook and bishop are not yet a battery, but the idea: maneuver the bishop to a long diagonal hitting g7/h8 behind the rook, then the rook gives a discovered check while the bishop covers escape squares. In real games this is the windmill engine — see [[windmill]].

## Common mistakes

- Forgetting that against a **double check the king MUST move** — players waste time looking for blocks or captures that are illegal.
- Letting the freed (front) piece move to a useless square; always ask "what is the best use of my free move?"
- Allowing the opponent a **counter-check** or capture that ends the discovery's tempo.
- Missing that your own discovered check can be **answered by capturing the checking piece** if it is undefended — verify the rear piece is safe.
- Overlooking discovered checks the OPPONENT has against you when you move a blocking piece.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train [Discovered Attack](https://lichess.org/training/discoveredAttack) and [Double Check](https://lichess.org/training/doubleCheck) — do 20 reps each, 3 sessions a week.
- **Pattern drill:** set up the smothered-mate sequence (Qg8+ Rxg8 Nf7#) from memory 10 times until automatic.
- **Windmill study:** replay **Torre–Lasker, Moscow 1925** (the famous Bf6!! windmill) and the **Nezhmetdinov** brilliancies.
- **Books:** *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) and *The Woodpecker Method* (Smith/Tikkanen) — repeat the discovery sets until clean.
- **Blindfold reps:** name the front piece, rear piece, and target square aloud for each puzzle to lock in recognition.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3B1PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Set up and execute a discovered check that wins the f7-pawn structure or improves decisively.
2. **FEN:** 6rk/6pp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3Q1RK1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Find the most forcing way to attack g8.
3. **FEN:** 5rk1/4Nppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Use the knight + rook battery idea to win the exchange.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. Reposition the bishop to the a7–g1 or a long diagonal so the e1-rook can later deliver a discovered check; the practical win is to bring the rook to e8 with tempo. The teaching answer: a discovered check frees the bishop to capture or fork while the king is in check.
2. **Qd8!** pins and overloads; if the rook leaves g8, mating ideas on the back rank appear. The most forcing tries are checks on the 8th rank backed by Rf1–f8.
3. **Re8!** — if **Rxe8** then **Nxe8** wins the exchange; the knight on e7 had also eyed g6/c6 forks, the discovered-check spirit of the position.

</details>

## Related

- [Discovered Attack](discovered-attack.md) — the non-checking parent motif.
- [Windmill](windmill.md) — repeated discovered checks harvesting material.
- [Smothered Mate](smothered-mate.md) — double-check king-trap mating net.
- [Pins](pin.md) and [Forks](fork.md) — common partners of the freed piece.

Tags: [[discovered-attack]] · [[double-check]] · [[windmill]] · [[smothered-mate]] · [[fork]]
